title: Registers and Basic ARM Instructions
summary: MOV, ADD, SUB, MUL, bitwise operations, shifts, CMP, and condition flags in ARM assembly.
content_html:
Registers and Basic ARM Instructions

ARM assembly programming centres on manipulating data in registers using a rich set of instructions. In this lesson, we cover the core data-processing instructions: moving data, arithmetic operations, bitwise logic, shifts, and comparison. Mastering these instructions is essential for writing any ARM assembly program.

Operand Types

ARM instructions can use three kinds of operands:

  • Register: One of R0-R15. Example: R3
  • Immediate: A constant value embedded in the instruction, prefixed with #. Example: #42. Not all immediate values are valid — they must be encodable in 12 bits (8-bit value rotated right by an even amount).
  • Shifted register: A register whose value is shifted before use. Example: R2, LSL #3 means R2 left-shifted by 3. This is powerful because shifting is “free” — it happens in the same clock cycle as the main operation via the barrel shifter.

MOV — Move

The MOV instruction loads a value into a destination register:

  • MOV R0, #5 — load the immediate value 5 into R0
  • MOV R1, R2 — copy the value of R2 into R1
  • MOV R0, R1, LSL #2 — load R1 shifted left by 2 into R0 (equivalent to R1 × 4)
  • MVN (Move Negative) loads the bitwise NOT of the operand: MVN R0, #0 loads 0xFFFFFFFF into R0.

ADD and SUB — Arithmetic

ADD adds two values and stores the result:

  • ADD R0, R1, R2 — R0 = R1 + R2
  • ADD R0, R0, #10 — R0 = R0 + 10
  • ADD R0, R1, R2, LSL #1 — R0 = R1 + (R2 × 2)

SUB subtracts:

  • SUB R0, R1, R2 — R0 = R1 − R2
  • SUB R0, R0, #1 — decrement R0
  • RSB (Reverse Subtract): RSB R0, R1, #0 computes R0 = 0 − R1 = −R1, useful for negation.

ADDS and SUBS are the flag-setting versions. They update the N, Z, C, V flags in CPSR. Without the S suffix, flags are not changed.

MUL — Multiply

MUL multiplies two registers:

  • MUL R0, R1, R2 — R0 = R1 × R2
  • Important: MUL does not accept immediate operands — both sources must be registers.
  • MLA (Multiply-Accumulate): MLA R0, R1, R2, R3 — R0 = R1 × R2 + R3. Very useful for dot products and polynomial evaluation.
  • UMULL/SMULL: Unsigned/signed multiply long — produces a 64-bit result in two registers.

Bitwise Logic Instructions

ARM provides bitwise operations that act on each bit independently:

  • AND: AND R0, R1, R2 — R0 = R1 AND R2 (bit masking — clear specific bits)
  • ORR: ORR R0, R1, R2 — R0 = R1 OR R2 (set specific bits)
  • EOR: EOR R0, R1, R2 — R0 = R1 XOR R2 (toggle specific bits)
  • BIC: BIC R0, R1, R2 — R0 = R1 AND NOT R2 (bit-clear; R2 specifies which bits to clear)
  • MVN: MVN R0, R1 — R0 = NOT R1 (bitwise complement)

Shift Instructions

Shifts move bits left or right within a register. They can be used as standalone instructions or as operand modifiers:

  • LSL (Logical Shift Left): LSL R0, R1, #n — R0 = R1 × 2n. Bits shifted out are lost; zeros fill from the right.
  • LSR (Logical Shift Right): LSR R0, R1, #n — R0 = R1 / 2n (unsigned division). Zeros fill from the left.
  • ASR (Arithmetic Shift Right): Same as LSR but fills with the sign bit (preserves sign for negative numbers).
  • ROR (Rotate Right): Bits shifted out reappear on the left side.

Shifts are extremely efficient for multiplication and division by powers of 2 and for extracting bit fields from packed data.

CMP — Compare

The CMP instruction subtracts one value from another, discards the result, but sets the CPSR flags:

  • CMP R0, #10 — computes R0 − 10, sets flags, discards result
  • CMP R1, R2 — computes R1 − R2, sets flags

Flags after CMP:

  • Z=1: operands were equal
  • N=1, V=0 (or N=0, V=1): first operand less than second (signed)
  • C=0: first operand less than second (unsigned)

CMN (Compare Negative) adds instead of subtracts. TST performs AND and sets flags (useful for testing specific bits). TEQ performs XOR and sets flags (test for equality without affecting V flag).

Condition Codes

Almost every ARM instruction can be made conditional by appending a two-letter condition code. Common codes: EQ (equal), NE (not equal), GT (signed greater than), GE (signed ≥), LT (signed less than), LE (signed ≤), HI/LO (unsigned higher/lower). Conditional execution eliminates many short branches. For example, MOVEQ R0, #1 loads 1 into R0 only if the last comparison found equality.

exercises.title: MOV Immediate
exercises.prompt: What does the instruction 'MOV R3, #0xFF' do?
options: Loads the value 255 into register R3
exercises.answer: Loads the value 255 into register R3

exercises.title: MUL Restriction
exercises.prompt: What is a key restriction of the ARM MUL instruction compared to ADD?
options: MUL can only multiply by powers of 2

exercises.title: LSL Operation
exercises.prompt: What is the result of 'LSL R0, R1, #3' if R1 contains the value 5?

exercises.title: CMP Instruction
exercises.prompt: After executing 'CMP R0, R1' where R0=10 and R1=10, which flag is set?
